substances.name: Alcohol
substances.notes: Explicitly advised against with ayahuasca in provided text.

substances.name: Amphetamine Stimulants
substances.notes: Explicitly high risk with MAOI context; potential hypertensive crisis/serotonin toxicity.

substances.name: Antihypertensives
substances.notes: Mentioned as risk area and also emergency management context.

substances.name: Antipsychotics
substances.notes: Mixed source signals; treat as elevated caution/high risk with ayahuasca.

substances.name: Atypical Antidepressants
substances.notes: Buspirone, trazodone, mirtazapine in psilocybin chart mostly blunted.

substances.name: Ayahuasca
substances.notes: Contains harmala alkaloids; interaction profile strongly MAOI-mediated.

substances.name: Belladonna
substances.notes: Marked distinct per request; no explicit risk ratings in provided documents.

substances.name: Benzodiazepines
substances.notes: Listed as generally low-risk emergency management option in supplied slides.

substances.name: Beta-Blockers
substances.notes: Rate-limiting beta-adrenergic antagonists; distinct from calcium-channel blockers.

substances.name: Beta-Blockers / Rate-Controlling CCBs
substances.notes: Aggregate node preserved for backward compatibility; superseded by beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers.

substances.name: Brugmansia
substances.notes: Marked distinct per request; no explicit risk ratings in provided documents.

substances.name: Calcium Channel Blockers
substances.notes: Distinct class from beta-blockers; includes agent-specific differences in conduction and vasodilation.

substances.name: Cannabis
substances.notes: Listed in lower-risk ayahuasca combinations.

substances.name: Clonidine
substances.notes: More sedating and hypotensive than guanfacine in many settings.

substances.name: Clonidine / Guanfacine
substances.notes: Aggregate node preserved for backward compatibility; superseded by clonidine and guanfacine.

substances.name: Cocaine
substances.notes: Contraindication list item with ayahuasca in source slides.

substances.name: DOx
substances.notes: Split from legacy aggregate node mdma_2cx_dox_nbome.

substances.name: 5-MeO-DMT
substances.notes: Specifically flagged as dangerous with MAOIs in supplied sources.

substances.name: Guanfacine
substances.notes: Similar class to clonidine but often somewhat less sedating.

substances.name: Kambo
substances.notes: Caution advised around co-presentation with ayahuasca.

substances.name: Ketamine
substances.notes: No formal ayahuasca interaction trials; concern is mainly confusion, sedation, and autonomic instability.

substances.name: Lamotrigine
substances.notes: Observational data suggest lower seizure concern than lithium with classic psychedelics.

substances.name: Lithium
substances.notes: Strong seizure signal reported in observational psychedelic co-use data.

substances.name: LSD
substances.notes: Included in lower-risk ayahuasca combination examples.

substances.name: Pharmaceutical MAOIs
substances.notes: Major contraindication category around ayahuasca and serotonergic combinations.

substances.name: MDMA
substances.notes: Split from legacy aggregate node mdma_2cx_dox_nbome.

substances.name: MDMA / 2C-x / DOx / NBOMe
substances.notes: Contraindication cluster with ayahuasca in source slides. Deprecated after entactogen split into mdma, two_c_x, dox, nbome_series.

substances.name: Mescaline / Peyote
substances.notes: Listed with spacing guidance relative to ayahuasca.

substances.name: Methylphenidate
substances.notes: Contraindication list item with ayahuasca in source slides.

substances.name: NBOMe Series
substances.notes: Split from legacy aggregate node mdma_2cx_dox_nbome.

substances.name: NDRI (Bupropion)
substances.notes: Psilocybin chart flags reduced seizure threshold and individualized risk.

substances.name: N,N-DMT
substances.notes: Referenced as generally lower risk with ayahuasca context than 5-MeO-DMT.

substances.name: Psilocybin Mushrooms
substances.notes: Classical psychedelic; medication interactions include blunting/intensification.

substances.name: Salvia divinorum
substances.notes: Marked distinct per request; no explicit risk ratings in provided documents.

substances.name: Serotonergic Opioids (Tramadol/Methadone/Meperidine/Tapentadol)
substances.notes: Contraindication list item with ayahuasca in source slides.

substances.name: SNRIs
substances.notes: Psilocybin chart: blunted effects; ayahuasca contraindication list includes SNRIs.

substances.name: SSRIs
substances.notes: Psilocybin chart: blunted effects; ayahuasca: serotonin syndrome risk.

substances.name: Tobacco / Rapé
substances.notes: Generally noted as acceptable with caveats on admixtures.

substances.name: Tricyclic Antidepressants
substances.notes: Psilocybin chart: intensified effects; specific tricyclics listed as contraindicated with ayahuasca.

substances.name: 2C-x
substances.notes: Split from legacy aggregate node mdma_2cx_dox_nbome.

substances.name: Yopo
substances.notes: Source notes caution due active constituents.

clinical_summary.headline: Caution interaction driven by synergistic impairment, nausea/vomiting, dehydration, accident risk, and unpredictable psychological effects.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Psilocybin and alcohol do not appear to create a classic receptor-level contraindication, but co-use can increase nausea, vomiting, dehydration, impaired coordination, tachycardia, blood-pressure changes, and behavioral risk. Psilocybin may dampen perceived alcohol intoxication for some users, encouraging heavier drinking and increasing risk of alcohol poisoning, falls, injuries, risky behavior, traffic accidents, or difficult psychological experiences.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: In therapeutic or ceremonial contexts, abstain from alcohol for at least one day before and after psilocybin.

source_refs.title: Choosingtherapy Psilocybin Alcohol
source_refs.notes: Matched via direct_pair source-linking from existing source library.

evidence.review_notes: Direct or high-confidence evidence supports this interaction.
evidence.evidence_gaps: Evidence is mostly from harm-reduction, clinical education, and broader alcohol/psychedelic literature rather than controlled co-ingestion studies. Systematic reviews do not identify a specific pharmacotoxic syndrome when co-ingested.

provenance.rationale: Caution interaction driven by synergistic impairment, nausea/vomiting, dehydration, accident risk, and unpredictable psychological effects.

clinical_summary.headline: Class-dependent cardiovascular caution; stable ACEi/ARB therapy is often less concerning than beta-blockers, clonidine, or rate-controlling calcium-channel blockers.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Ayahuasca commonly raises heart rate and blood pressure while also causing vomiting, diarrhea, and fluid shifts. Background antihypertensive therapy can make those hemodynamic swings less predictable, especially if compensatory tachycardia is blunted or the participant becomes volume depleted.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: If recent vomiting, diarrhea, fasting, heat exposure, or repeated cups are involved, the interaction risk rises because volume depletion makes antihypertensives harder to titrate safely.

clinical_summary.mechanism: Both drugs can shape blood pressure or pulse, so the main issue is compensatory load rather than a unique toxidrome.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Monitor for transient blood pressure and heart-rate elevation during onset and peak psychedelic effects, followed by possible postural hypotension, bradycardia, dizziness, or syncope later in the session or post-acute period.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Do not abruptly stop prescribed antihypertensives before psychedelic use. People with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or multi-drug antihypertensive regimens should have cardiovascular screening and BP/HR monitoring.

clinical_summary.headline: Usually a psychiatric contraindication rather than a classic serotonergic-toxicity problem; many antipsychotics will also strongly suppress DMT effects.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Many antipsychotics are potent 5-HT2A antagonists, so they can flatten DMT effects while adding their own burdens such as orthostasis, QT issues, sedation, or metabolic strain. The larger concern is that the person is often being treated for psychosis-spectrum illness, bipolar instability, or severe agitation.

clinical_summary.headline: Tropane-rich deliriants such as belladonna and Brugmansia should be treated as contraindicated with psychedelics because anticholinergic toxidrome and psychedelic disorganization are a dangerous mix.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Belladonna and Brugmansia contain atropine-, scopolamine-, and hyoscyamine-like alkaloids that can produce tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium, urinary retention, arrhythmia, and profound confusion. Combining that with serotonergic psychedelic effects is a worst-case operational scenario.

clinical_summary.headline: Emergency benzodiazepine use is generally low-risk and clinically preferred for severe agitation; planned co-use more often blunts effects and complicates screening than it causes direct toxicity.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Benzodiazepines are GABAergic rather than serotonergic, which is why toxicology guidance favors them for agitation, muscle rigidity, and serotonin-toxicity management. The main tradeoffs are sedation, falls, aspiration during heavy purge, and a flatter psychedelic process.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Stable low-dose maintenance mainly predicts effect blunting. Escalating doses, erratic use, or recent withdrawal raise the risk profile much more than the pharmacology of the pair itself.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Field consensus is often stricter than toxicology literature: many retreats discourage baseline benzo use but still keep benzodiazepines available as emergency rescue medication.

clinical_summary.mechanism: Kambo produces short, intense autonomic stress with vomiting, hypotension, tachycardia, and fluid/electrolyte loss. Ayahuasca adds further blood-pressure shifts, purging, and cardiovascular activation, so the main risk is somatic rather than serotonergic.

clinical_summary.headline: Caution interaction with low confidence, driven by plausible additive blood-pressure/heart-rate load, serotonergic-stack concerns, and dissociation plus purge-related operational risk.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Ayahuasca combines DMT with reversible harmala MAO-A inhibition, creating serotonergic psychedelic effects, monoamine elevation, and moderate blood-pressure and heart-rate increases. Ketamine primarily acts as an NMDA antagonist but can acutely increase blood pressure and heart rate through sympathetic activation and monoaminergic effects. Ketamine plus pharmaceutical MAOIs has produced mostly mild transient BP/HR rises in small monitored clinical series, without clear serotonin-syndrome or hypertensive-crisis signals, but ayahuasca plus ketamine has not been directly studied. During overlapping effects, additive hemodynamic load, serotonergic polypharmacy risk, dissociation, vomiting, falls, aspiration, confusion, and impaired help-seeking are the main concerns.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Avoid ketamine for at least 2–3 days after ayahuasca as a conservative spacing heuristic, especially when other serotonergic drugs are present.

clinical_summary.headline: This is the basic ayahuasca/pharmahuasca interaction at oral doses, but layering extra smoked or vaped DMT on top of ayahuasca meaningfully raises intensity and can become unsafe at high combined loads.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Ayahuasca's harmala alkaloids reversibly inhibit MAO-A and prolong DMT exposure. The main issue is not a mysterious new toxic syndrome but sharply increased total psychedelic load, longer duration, and bigger cardiovascular and behavioral swings when extra DMT is added during peak ayahuasca.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Ayahuasca should still be treated as conferring meaningful MAO-A inhibition for about 24 hours, with a more conservative 24-48 hour washout after heavy or repeated dosing.

provenance.rationale: Not clearly toxic in the way ayahuasca plus SSRIs or MDMA can be, but same-session overlap is a strong intensifier and can become operationally unsafe.

clinical_summary.headline: Salvia is non-serotonergic, but same-session use with classic psychedelics can become unsafe because disorientation, dysphoria, and accident risk stack badly.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Salvinorin A is a kappa-opioid agonist rather than a classic serotonergic psychedelic. The overlap is at autonomic activation and severe state disruption, not receptor-level serotonin toxicity.

clinical_summary.headline: Traditional low-frequency use appears broadly compatible in healthy people, but repeated or heavy tobacco/rapé use can add meaningful blood-pressure and anxiety load.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Nicotine raises heart rate and blood pressure through catecholamine release. Ayahuasca can do the same, so the combination is usually tolerated in healthy participants but can become a problem with cardiovascular disease, naïve users, or repeated strong applications.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Traditional Amazonian practice is one reason this pair should not be overclassified, but facilitator experience still supports caution around repeated dosing and admixtures.

clinical_summary.headline: Concurrent ayahuasca plus yopo should be treated as contraindicated because bufotenine/5-substituted tryptamine exposure under MAO inhibition is poorly studied and plausibly high-risk.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Yopo preparations are often bufotenine-dominant and may contain small amounts of DMT or 5-MeO-DMT depending on material and preparation. Ayahuasca's MAO-A inhibition can increase exposure to 5-substituted tryptamines, which is exactly the class that underground and ethnographic guidance treats most cautiously.

clinical_summary.headline: Tropane-rich deliriants such as belladonna and Brugmansia should be treated as contraindicated with psychedelics because anticholinergic toxidrome and psychedelic disorganization are a dangerous mix.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Belladonna and Brugmansia contain atropine-, scopolamine-, and hyoscyamine-like alkaloids that can produce tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium, urinary retention, arrhythmia, and profound confusion. Combining that with serotonergic psychedelic effects is a worst-case operational scenario.

clinical_summary.headline: Beta blockers and LSD are likely clinically manageable but under-studied; caution is warranted due to cardiovascular overlap and limited direct evidence.
clinical_summary.mechanism: LSD can transiently increase blood pressure and heart rate. Beta blockers may blunt heart-rate increases but may not fully prevent pressor effects, may mask adrenergic warning signs, and may contribute to hypotension or bradycardia later in the experience.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Do not abruptly discontinue prescribed beta blockers. People with hypertension, arrhythmias, structural heart disease, or post-MI history should avoid unsupervised LSD use.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Avoid high-dose LSD, stimulants, and vasoconstrictors. Seek urgent care for chest pain, severe headache, dyspnoea, presyncope, or sustained palpitations.

clinical_summary.headline: Tropane-rich deliriants such as belladonna and Brugmansia should be treated as contraindicated with psychedelics because anticholinergic toxidrome and psychedelic disorganization are a dangerous mix.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Belladonna and Brugmansia contain atropine-, scopolamine-, and hyoscyamine-like alkaloids that can produce tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium, urinary retention, arrhythmia, and profound confusion. Combining that with serotonergic psychedelic effects is a worst-case operational scenario.

clinical_summary.headline: Tropane-rich deliriants such as belladonna and Brugmansia should be treated as contraindicated with psychedelics because anticholinergic toxidrome and psychedelic disorganization are a dangerous mix.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Belladonna and Brugmansia contain atropine-, scopolamine-, and hyoscyamine-like alkaloids that can produce tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium, urinary retention, arrhythmia, and profound confusion. Combining that with serotonergic psychedelic effects is a worst-case operational scenario.

clinical_summary.headline: Caution interaction driven by intensified and prolonged LSD effects with increased risk of anxiety, paranoia, psychosis-like reactions, impaired judgment, and accidents.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Cannabis can intensify and prolong LSD-related hallucinations, perceptual distortions, anxiety, paranoia, confusion, and impaired judgment, especially in people prone to mental health problems. Observational data on cannabis co-use with classic psychedelics show dose-dependent increases in mystical-type effects, visual phenomena, ego dissolution, and, at higher cannabis doses, challenging experiences. Serious medical toxicity appears rare, but psychological destabilization and hazardous behavior are plausible risks.

clinical_summary.headline: Caution interaction driven by dose-dependent intensification of psilocybin effects and increased risk of challenging experiences, anxiety, paranoia, or destabilization.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Cannabis co-use with classic psychedelics is common in real-world settings. Prospective survey data suggest simultaneous cannabis use can dose-dependently increase mystical-type effects, visual phenomena, and ego dissolution, while higher cannabis doses may also increase challenging experiences. The main risk is psychological and operational rather than severe physiological toxicity: cannabis may intensify or prolong psilocybin effects and increase anxiety, paranoia, derealization, or severe bad-trip reactions in vulnerable individuals.

clinical_summary.headline: Opposing autonomic effects with possible intensity modulation
clinical_summary.mechanism: LSD can increase blood pressure and heart rate during the acute experience, while clonidine and guanfacine reduce sympathetic tone through central alpha-2 agonism. The pair may partially offset cardiovascular stimulation but also increase the chance of later hypotension, bradycardia, sedation, and altered psychedelic intensity.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Expect early increases in blood pressure and heart rate followed by possible later hypotension or bradycardia as alpha-2 agonist effects dominate.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Use a supervised low-stimulation setting because dizziness, impaired coordination, and an unpredictable intensity shift are plausible.

clinical_summary.headline: A CNS-depressant or opioid-like partner with another depressant signal suggests pharmacodynamic CNS depression with possible respiratory involvement.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Sedation, slowed respiration, vomiting, and impaired self-protection can stack even when the exact chemistry differs.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Avoid Kambo during active serotonergic opioid use and around periods of opioid dose escalation, intoxication, withdrawal, or concurrent sedative/serotonergic drug use.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Strongly avoid Kambo in people currently taking serotonergic opioids, especially tramadol, tapentadol, meperidine, dextromethorphan, methadone, or fentanyl, and avoid especially in unsupervised ceremonial settings. Chronic opioid patients should discuss any contemplated Kambo use with a physician rather than attempting self-tapering or switching.

clinical_summary.headline: Caution interaction with low confidence, driven by plausible additive dissociation, psychedelic intensification, cardiovascular activation, confusion, and accident risk.
clinical_summary.mechanism: LSD is a high-potency, long-acting 5-HT2A agonist psychedelic, while ketamine is a dissociative NMDA antagonist that increases glutamatergic signaling and cortical entropy. Both substances can increase measures of cortical complexity and alter ego boundaries, perception, and cognition. Co-use may produce additive or supra-additive psychological disruption, dissociation, ataxia, tachycardia, elevated blood pressure, anxiety, panic, transient psychosis-like reactions, impaired self-care, and accident risk.

source_refs.title: Ketamine Psychiatric Medication Interactions Ijnp
source_refs.notes: Matched via direct_pair source-linking from existing source library.

evidence.review_notes: Direct or high-confidence evidence supports this interaction.
evidence.evidence_gaps: There is virtually no systematic clinical literature on intentional LSD plus ketamine co-administration; current risk estimates are based on individual drug profiles, shared glutamatergic/cortical-complexity mechanisms, and scattered anecdotal or theoretical considerations.

provenance.rationale: Caution interaction with low confidence, driven by plausible additive dissociation, psychedelic intensification, cardiovascular activation, confusion, and accident risk.

clinical_summary.headline: An NMDA-antagonist pair with a classic psychedelic suggests glutamate modulation and dissociative synergy rather than a clearly defined toxic syndrome.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Ketamine-like NMDA antagonism can overlap with serotonergic psychedelic effects through glutamatergic modulation, dissociation, and altered perception.

clinical_summary.headline: Salvia is non-serotonergic, but same-session use with classic psychedelics can become unsafe because disorientation, dysphoria, and accident risk stack badly.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Salvinorin A is a kappa-opioid agonist rather than a classic serotonergic psychedelic. The overlap is at autonomic activation and severe state disruption, not receptor-level serotonin toxicity.

clinical_summary.headline: Serotonin-toxicity and overdose risks rise in poly-drug settings
clinical_summary.mechanism: Some opioids, especially tramadol, tapentadol, dextromethorphan, meperidine, fentanyl, and methadone, can add serotonergic burden or impair respiration and cognition. LSD is a classic serotonergic psychedelic and is not a strong serotonin reuptake inhibitor, so the main concern is additive risk in broader serotonergic stacks plus overlapping autonomic and behavioral impairment.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Risk monitoring should cover the full psychedelic session and post-acute period, with attention to mental status, temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and neuromuscular signs.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Avoid using serotonergic opioids as ad-hoc trip smoothers or pain control during a psychedelic experience. Prefer non-serotonergic analgesic strategies where medically appropriate.

clinical_summary.headline: Salvia is non-serotonergic, but same-session use with classic psychedelics can become unsafe because disorientation, dysphoria, and accident risk stack badly.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Salvinorin A is a kappa-opioid agonist rather than a classic serotonergic psychedelic. The overlap is at autonomic activation and severe state disruption, not receptor-level serotonin toxicity.

source_refs.title: Overview Psilocybin Lsd Mdma Ketamine 2025
source_refs.notes: Matched via adjacent_domain source-linking from existing source library.

evidence.review_notes: Direct or high-confidence evidence supports this interaction.
evidence.evidence_gaps: Formal combination studies are absent, so this remains a precautionary field rule.

provenance.rationale: Salvia is non-serotonergic, but same-session use with classic psychedelics can become unsafe because disorientation, dysphoria, and accident risk stack badly.

clinical_summary.headline: Serotonin-toxicity and overdose risks rise in poly-drug settings
clinical_summary.mechanism: Some opioids, especially tramadol, tapentadol, dextromethorphan, meperidine, fentanyl, and methadone, can add serotonergic burden or impair respiration and cognition. Mescaline is a classic serotonergic psychedelic and is not a strong serotonin reuptake inhibitor, so the main concern is additive risk in broader serotonergic stacks plus overlapping autonomic and behavioral impairment.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Risk monitoring should cover the full psychedelic session and post-acute period, with attention to mental status, temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and neuromuscular signs.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Avoid using serotonergic opioids as ad-hoc trip smoothers or pain control during a psychedelic experience. Prefer non-serotonergic analgesic strategies where medically appropriate.

clinical_summary.mechanism: Additive catecholaminergic and serotonergic activation can raise heart rate, blood pressure, anxiety, and overheating risk.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Risk is likely highest during and shortly after rapé administration, when nicotine levels, autonomic activation, nausea, vomiting, and dehydration risk are greatest.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Treat rapé as a strong stimulant when using bupropion. Use only small test doses if used at all, avoid repeated or high-intensity applications, and avoid combining with other stimulants such as amphetamines, cocaine, or high caffeine. Avoid rapé when sleep-deprived, dehydrated, fasting heavily, or otherwise seizure-prone. Seek urgent care for seizure, loss of consciousness, chest pain, severe headache, focal neurological symptoms, or collapse.

clinical_summary.headline: Serotonin-toxicity and overdose risks rise in poly-drug settings
clinical_summary.mechanism: Some opioids, especially tramadol, tapentadol, dextromethorphan, meperidine, fentanyl, and methadone, can add serotonergic burden or impair respiration and cognition. Psilocybin is a classic serotonergic psychedelic and is not a strong serotonin reuptake inhibitor, so the main concern is additive risk in broader serotonergic stacks plus overlapping autonomic and behavioral impairment.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Risk monitoring should cover the full psychedelic session and post-acute period, with attention to mental status, temperature, blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and neuromuscular signs.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Avoid using serotonergic opioids as ad-hoc trip smoothers or pain control during a psychedelic experience. Prefer non-serotonergic analgesic strategies where medically appropriate.

clinical_summary.headline: A CNS-depressant or opioid-like partner with another depressant signal suggests pharmacodynamic CNS depression with possible respiratory involvement.
clinical_summary.mechanism: Sedation, slowed respiration, vomiting, and impaired self-protection can stack even when the exact chemistry differs.
clinical_summary.timing_guidance: Risk is likely highest during acute rapé effects and periods of opioid intoxication, dose escalation, withdrawal, or concurrent CNS depressant use.
clinical_summary.field_notes: Avoid heavy or ritualised rapé use while taking serotonergic opioids, especially tramadol, tapentadol, meperidine, dextromethorphan, methadone, or fentanyl. Use extra caution if serotonergic antidepressants, MAOIs, alcohol, benzodiazepines, or other CNS depressants are also present. If used at all, keep doses low and infrequent, remain upright, and use sober supervision. Treat severe sedation, slow or irregular breathing, cyanosis, chest pain, collapse, or unresponsiveness as an emergency.
